subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Henrietta Leavitt and Cosmic Distances
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Henrietta Leavitt and Cosmic Distances.

hook.text: Henrietta Leavitt discovered a way to measure the universe's size. She was paid 25 cents an hour for the work.

buildup.text: She studied variable stars in the Magellanic Clouds, finding that brighter Cepheids pulse more slowly.

discovery.text: This period-luminosity relationship became the first reliable method for measuring distances to other galaxies.

twist.text: Leavitt was a 'computer'—a woman hired to do tedious calculations men didn't want. She transformed astronomy from that role.

climax.text: Hubble used her discovery to prove the universe is expanding. Without Leavitt's work, modern cosmology couldn't exist.

punchline.text: Twenty-five cents an hour. She gave us the ruler to measure the cosmos.

quiz.question: What did Henrietta Leavitt discover about Cepheid variable stars?
options: Brighter Cepheids pulse more slowly—a relationship used to measure cosmic distances
They are the oldest stars in the universe
They always appear in pairs orbiting each other
